title: Agent Context Memory: A Governance-First Specification for AI Agent Context Memory
description:
What this is. A specification for governance-first context memory in autonomous AI agent systems.

The contribution. ACM recognizes that agent memory is fundamentally an authorization problem and applies established access control patterns — the "authorization before action" principle common to RBAC, MAC, and capability-based security — to this domain. The core contribution is the Mandate Gate: a pre-work mandate, authored by the principal, must exist in the agent's memory before any session of work is valid. Prior memory models (MemGPT, CoALA, Generative Agents) treat memory as a working resource the agent draws from during work. ACM treats memory as a precondition — the gate that determines whether work can begin at all.

Why it matters. The question is not only how agents use memory effectively, but on what evidence a principal should trust that the agent was authorized to act and that its account of what happened is reliable.

Relationship to PEA. ACM is the operationalization of Principles of Earned Autonomy (PEA) for the context memory design problem. PEA is the theory; ACM is the implementation standard for memory.

Core structure. Two orthogonal governance axes: (1) Trust axis — three memory tiers organized by trust level (not memory type): Intent (principal-authored mandate), Trace (agent decisions, append-only), Evidence (harness-captured session records); (2) Scope axis — memory can exist at nested scopes (repo → workspace → org), higher-scope mandates govern lower-scope ones, scopes discovered by parent-directory traversal with explicit stop conditions (§4.2). Structural requirements: mandate gate, capture-author separation, append-only trace, trust-tiered conflict resolution, scope hierarchy. Convergence defined as a memory-level property.

What is new in v0.2.0. Section 4 (Scoped Memory) adds the scope axis as a core governance dimension — orthogonal to the trust axis, not subordinate. Stop conditions for scope traversal are formally defined: filesystem root (hard stop), .acm-root marker file (operator-declared ceiling), 4-level implementation ceiling (RECOMMENDED).

Prior art positioning. ACM acknowledges RBAC/MAC/capability-based security and audit-log principles as established mechanisms. Formal citations to CoALA (arXiv:2309.02427), Generative Agents (arXiv:2304.03442), and MemGPT (arXiv:2310.08560). The contribution is transferring these patterns to the agent memory domain.

Relationship to AII. ACM provides infrastructure for Augmented Individual Intelligence (AII) by structurally enforcing the agency-preservation boundary condition that AII requires.

Reference implementation. The .acm/ directory pattern, publicly released April 24, 2026 (DOI: 10.5281/zenodo.19732827) before this specification was written. The implementation predates and informed the specification.

Read in this order.

  1. README.md — overview and elevator pitch
  2. SPEC.md — the full specification (Sections 1-6 + Appendices)
  3. .acm/destination.md — the operator-held mandate for this spec's own development
